texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas separation efficiencies of three zeolite membranes s Faujasite, MFI, and Chabazite d have been examined using the method of molecular dynamics. Our investigation has allowed us to study the effects of pore size and structure, state conditions, and compositions on the permeation of two binary gas mixtures, O 2 / N 2 and CO2 /N2. We have found that for the mixture components with similar sizes and adsorption characteristics, such as O 2 / N 2 , small-pore zeolites are not suited for separations, and this result is explicable at the molecular level. For mixture components with differing adsorption behavior, such as CO 2 / N 2 , separation is mainly governed by adsorption and small-pore zeolites separate such gases quite efficiently. When selective adsorption takes place, we have found that, for species with low adsorption, the permeation rate is low, even if the diffusion rate is quite high. Our results further indicate that loading s adsorption d dominates the separation of gas mixtures in small-pore zeolites, such as MFI and Chabazite. For larger-pore zeolites such as Faujasite, diffusion rates do have some effect on gas mixture separation, although adsorption continues to be important. Finally, our simulations using existing intermolecular potential models have replicated all known experimental results for these systems. This shows that molecular simulations could serve as a useful screening tool to determine the suitability of a membrane for potential separation applications. © 2005 American Institute of Physics . f DOI: 10.1063/1.1930829 g

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Advanced gas separation techniques based on zeolite membranes offer considerable cost improvement and fewer negative environmental consequences compared to traditional cryogenic methods. However, further development of such methods requires a better understanding of both the dynamic s diffusion d and the equilibrium s adsorption d behavior of gaseous mixtures in zeolite crystals. Moleculardynamics methods have been used here to provide such information by examining the molecular level forces between the competing gas molecules and the membrane surfaces responsible for these separations. In a previous study, we reported molecular dynamics simulation studies of gas separations with Faujasite zeolite membrane. 1 In this study, we have studied smaller-pore s MFI and Chabazite d zeolites. This has allowed us to examine the role of pore size, molecular forces, state condition, etc., on the separation efficiency.

texts.orig: Silicalite and ZSM-5, which are MFI-type structures, are the most widely used zeolites for membranes. Silicalite is a pure silica zeolite with a system of straight channels interconnected by zigzag channels. texts.orig: Understanding the separation mechanism of zeolites is not a simple matter. There are often unexpected results in binary mixtures if an attempt is made to predict these based upon the experimental behavior of pure systems. In this paper we report computer simulation studies using a technique developed by us 4-7 to model the separation processes based on an extension of the NVT molecular-dynamics method. 8 The molecules s that permeate the membrane d are recycled periodically to model the steady-state flow. 9

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The objective of this work is to study the temperature, pressure and composition effects on the permeation and separation of gaseous mixtures using zeolite membranes and explain this behavior at the molecular level. The zeolites chosen exhibit a range of behavior. For example, Faujasite is N2 selective for both pure N2 and O2, and their mixtures, while Chabazite is O 2 selective. Faujasite is CO 2 selective in both pure CO2 and N2 and their mixtures, while Chabazite is N2 selective in pure gases, and CO 2 selective in mixtures. If simulations using currently available potential models s without further adjustments d could accurately represent this behavior, they could potentially be used as an inexpensive screening tool to determine the suitability of a membrane for a potential gas separation problem. Finally, electric fields have also been investigated to determine if these can be used to improve the separation efficiency significantly.

texts.text: III. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In our MD simulations, the separation of two gas mixtures s N2/O2 and N2 /CO2 d was studied using three zeolite s MFI, Chabazite, and Faujasite d membranes. These mixtures were selected because they show divergent behavior in going from single s pure d system to binary system in some experimental studies. In one case the ideal separation factor is larger than the mixture separation factor, while the opposite behavior in the second case. Three zeolites were selected for their different pore sizes, which vary between approximately 4-7 Å, and structure s see Table II d . These pore sizes cover the range where the role of diffusion and adsorption can vary significantly and this leads to these zeolites exhibiting a range of selectivities for the mixtures being studied. Results for Faujasite have been reported previously; 1 we will therefore only summarize those results and refer to them when needed for comparison purposes only. The ratio of silicon to aluminum was assumed to be infinity in all three zeolites.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The MFI zeolite membrane s see Fig. 1 d has an anisotropic bidimensional and bimodal channel network; 2 straight channels of elliptical cross sections s 5.1 3 5.7 Å d extending along the y direction which are cross-linked with sinusoidal channels of nearly circular cross section s 5.4 Å d extending in the x direction. Mass transport in the z direction can succeed only if the gases move repeatedly in succession in these two types of pores. Adsorption studies have shown, however, that molecules larger than the x-ray diffraction s XRD d pore diameters can fit inside the silicalite pore. This is because the pores 'breathe,' a feature included in our model albeit somewhat simplistically. The effective H-ZSM-5 pore size has been calculated as a function of temperature. 16 It is reported that at 573 K, the pore size varies between 6.6 and 7.3 Å.

Chabazite is one of the most porous natural zeolites. 17 Cations occupy four sites according to the scheme of Calligaris and Nardin. 3 Site I is on a triad in the large cavity next to a hexagonal prism. Site II is also on a triad, almost in the center of a large cavity while site III is located on a triad at the center of a hexagonal prism. Only small bivalent cations are located at this site. texts.orig: Faujasite has a three-dimensional pore structure with pores running perpendicular to each other in the x , y , and z planes similar to Linde type A s LTA d , and is made of secondary building units 4, 6, and 6-6. The pore diameter is large at 7.4 Å since the aperture is defined by a 12-member oxygen ring, and leads into a larger cavity of diameter 12 Å. The cavity is surrounded by ten sodalite cages s truncated octahedra d connected on their hexagonal faces. The unit cell is cubic s a =24.965 Å d with Fd3m symmetry.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our previous simulation studies have shown that the permeation rate of gases in zeolites is primarily determined by two rate-determining factors: s i d Diffusion of the gases through the zeolite pores. The diffusion regimes are usually determined by two factors, viz., the ratio of the channel diameter of the zeolite to molecular diameter, and the ratio of molecular mean free path to molecular diameter. s ii d Loading of the gases inside the zeolite membranes. This is primarily determined by the adsorption isotherms of the gases. 2 Both of these two factors can be influenced by the temperature, pressure, interaction with adsorption sites, and other molecules inside the membrane. We will discuss the results presented in the section in the context of these two important rate-determining factors.

It is also clear from Fig. 2 s a d that the pressure dependence of the permeance changes from a strongly nonlinear dependence at 242 K to an almost linear dependence when the temperature increases to 403 K. These results can be explained by examining the diffusion rates and loadings in the membrane. When the temperature increases, for example, for N 2 from 242 to 323 K, the mean-square displacement s MSD d in the x direction s perpendicular to the membrane surface d at the end of simulation s this is related to self-diffusion coefficient, see discussion to follow d increases from 835 to 1080, but the loading s defined as the average number of molecules inside the membrane d decreases from 50 to 41. The effect of the decrease in the loading is slightly more dominant, so the permeance therefore goes down from 0.38 3 10 7 to 0.35 3 10 7 mol/ s m 2 s Pa d . As for the pressure effect, when the temperature is low s such as 242 and 323 K d , the loading is relatively unchanged when pressure is increased. This indicates that the coverage of adsorbed molecules on zeolite surface is very high. When the coverage is high and the temperature is low, permeation is primarily driven by surface diffusion, which explains the decrease in permeance, since the rate of surface diffusion decreases as the pressure is increased. At higher temperatures the pressure dependence of the permeance is much weaker. Mass transport by surface diffusion takes place by molecules jumping between adsorption sites. When the temperature increases the velocity of molecules goes up. Thus the mean-square displacement increases at higher temperature. For example, the MSD in- creases to 1100 from 903 when the temperature increases to 323 from 242 K for O2 at a reduced density of 0.03. The kinetic energy of molecules increases with temperature while the interaction with the adsorption sites remains essentially constant. Consequently, the mass transport mechanism will transform from surface diffusion to Knudsen-like diffusion, which makes the pressure dependence of permeance weaker. We have observed that if the temperature increases, once the kinetic energy of gas molecules overcomes the interaction with the adsorption site, the permeance becomes almost independent of pressure, which is characteristic of Knudsen diffusion.

texts.orig: The first term in Eq. s 5 d is the surface diffusion contribution, the second term is Darcy's law term for viscous flow, and the third term is the Knudsen contribution. We examined the nature of permeance in the N 2 / O 2 by fitting our results to Eq. s 5 d . At low temperatures, parameters B and C are negligible, which shows that Knudsen and viscous flow do not contribute. At higher temperatures, our simulation results fit the Knudsen model quite well with parameters A and B negligible. These results show that the dominant mechanism for N2/O2 permeation shifts from surface diffusion to the Knudsen diffusion when temperature increases. This analysis agrees with our previous discussion based on MSD and loading of the membrane.

This is primarily responsible for the drop in separation factor in mixtures compared to Faujasite zeolite. The MSD for gas mixture as a function of time in the x direction is shown in Fig. 5. The MSD reported here is the average over time, irrespective of whether the molecules are inside or outside the membrane, as is usually done in such simulations. 4-9 However, since the diffusion rate inside the membrane is much smaller than that outside the membrane s here bulk gas d , this number effectively represents the controlling step, which is diffusion inside the membrane. For example, if a specie was unable to enter the membrane s because of its size d , then the MSD of a specie would initially increase to represent diffusion between the two membrane surfaces, but then cease to increase, and for t → ' the diffusion rate would be zero. Because we use periodic boundary conditions in our simulations, MSD is a linear function of time during the entire simulation, and the effective diffusion coefficient is proportional to the slope of MSD with time. We can observe from Fig. 5 that the MSD of oxygen and nitrogen in binary mixtures and pure systems are comparable. It therefore appears oxygen and nitrogen in mixtures do not influence each other's diffusion rates in the MFI zeolite membranes significantly. This behavior incidentally is, again, quite different from our observations in a Faujasite membrane reported previously. 1 These differences will be discussed further in Sec. IV B. texts.orig: There are very few experimental results available for Chabazite membranes. Therefore we carried out only a few simulations on the effect of pressure on the performance of Chabazite membranes. Experimental data indicate, however, that the Chabazite is oxygen selective, compared to Faujasite which is nitrogen selective. One goal of this investigation was to determine if our simulation can replicate this behavior, and also explain the reasons for this changed selectivity. The results for the permeance of oxygen and nitrogen as pure gases and equimolar binary mixtures at a temperature of 323 K and pressures up to 500 bars are shown in Fig. 7. It can be observed from the reported results that the permeance in all cases decreases with pressure as in MFI zeolites. The permeance is smaller compared to MFI zeolite, which can be explained by the fact that the pore size of Chabazite is smaller than the pore size of MFI. Unlike pure N2 and O2 in Faujasite, the MSD of oxygen is much higher than the MSD of nitrogen and Chabazite is therefore as a consequence oxygen selective. The pore size of Chabazite is 3.8 Å and the kinetic diameters for oxygen and nitrogen are 3.46 and 3.64 Å, respectively. Since the kinetic diameters of oxygen and nitrogen are very close to the pore size of Chabazite, they have a significant effect on the diffusion rate of oxygen and nitrogen. The smaller molecule oxygen moves faster than the larger molecule nitrogen, which is responsible for the oxygen selectivity of Chabazite.

The drop can be explained by comparing the mean-square displacement and loading for oxygen and nitrogen in both pure systems and mixtures. The MSD for pure gases and gas mixture as a function of time is shown in Fig. 1S s Supplementary section d . 21 For binary mixtures, there is slightly selective loading of oxygen, 24 for oxygen and 21 for nitrogen, while the total loading appears to be constant. The MSD of oxygen drops down from 775 to 565, as can be seen in Fig. 1S, while the mean square displacement of nitrogen increases a little bit from 563 to 594. This is because the pore size is comparable to the molecular diameters of oxygen and nitrogen and they cannot cross each other in the zeolite. This leads to the observed drop in the selectivity. The slightly selective loading of oxygen is responsible for the slight O 2 selectivity.

texts.orig: The results for the permeance of carbon dioxide and nitrogen in equimolar binary mixtures at temperature 323 K are shown in Fig. 10. We observe that the mixture has a much higher selectivity than the pure gases, which is also observed experimentally. 22,23 Our results for both ideal and mixture selectivity have been compared with experiments in Fig. 11. The comparisons show qualitative agreement, including the increase in the separation factor and the decrease in the permeance of N 2 in going from pure systems to binary systems. The MSD of CO2 and N2 at 323 K are shown in Fig. 2S. The MSD of CO2 increases from 626 to 770 while that of N 2 decreases from 1020 to 881 in going from a pure system to a binary system. The MSD of N2 is still slightly higher than the MSD of CO2, but the loading for pure CO 2 and N2 is 46 and 44, while the loading for CO 2 and N2 in the mixture is 29 and 23, respectively. Thus, although the MSD of nitrogen continues to be higher than that of carbon dioxide, there is clearly more selective loading of carbon dioxide in the membrane, which explains the much higher selectivity in mixtures.

texts.orig: B. Chabazite zeolite membranes
texts.text: B. Chabazite zeolite membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several simulations were carried out to investigate the performance of Chabazite membranes for the separation of CO2/N2 mixtures. The results for the permeation of carbon dioxide and nitrogen as pure gases and equimolar mixtures for a temperature 323 K and pressures up to 500 bars are shown in Fig. 13. For the pure gases, more nitrogen molecules permeate the membrane than carbon dioxide at similar pressures, but for mixtures, carbon dioxide has a higher permeance. As shown in Fig. 3S, the MSD of nitrogen is higher than the MSD of carbon dioxide in both pure gases and mixtures, and the MSD of both nitrogen and carbon dioxide drop in going from pure gases to a mixtures. Here once again, it is the loading that plays a more important role in the mixture separation. The loadings for nitrogen and carbon dioxide in pure gases are 51 and 42, but in the mixture they are 10 and 42, respectively, which explains the significant increase in the selectivity of carbon dioxide observed, as well as the reversal in the selectivity.

texts.orig: We also examined the MSD and loading of the membrane in the presence of an electric field to understand the observed permeance results. From Tables V and VI we observe that for both O 2 / N 2 and CO2 /N2 systems s both pure and equimolar mixtures d , the loading decreases in the presence of electric field while the diffusion rate increases. For example, in the case of oxygen, when the electric field changes from 0 to 0.05, the loading decreases to 17 from 24 while the MSD increases to 1185 from 1010. These two effects essentially cancel out, so we do not observe any changes in the permeance. For carbon dioxide, when the electric field changes from 0 to 0.1, the loading decreases to 6 from 24 while the MSD increases to 1083 from 842. The effect of the decrease in loading dominates the increase of MSD s diffusivity d , so there is a decrease in the permeance of carbon dioxide. For both CO2 /N 2 and O2 /N2 mixtures the results are comparable to those for pure gases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For both mixtures all components have similar diffusion rates s within 20% d for the three zeolites studied here except for CO2/N2 in Faujasite. This is because molecules cannot easily cross each other due to small pores, except for Faujasite s with the largest pores d where this is occasionally possible. The selectivities are therefore determined by the loadings. For O 2 / N 2 mixtures, since both O 2 and N2 have similar loadings, the separation factors for all three zeolites are quite low. Faujasite is N 2 selective because of the higher loading of N2 and MFI and Chabazite are O 2 selective because of the selective loading of O 2 . For CO2 /N2 mixtures all three have higher CO2 loadings, and are therefore all CO 2 selective especially Faujasite and MFI. It should be noted that in the case of Chabazite, the ideal separation factor favors N2, while in mixtures the membrane favors CO2 .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: VIII. CONCLUSION
texts.text: VIII. CONCLUSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With the availability of fast computers at relatively low cost, molecular dynamics can be a very useful tool for understanding membrane-based separations at the fundamental molecular level. Our results have also shown that molecular simulation can be a very powerful tool for carrying out preliminary screening studies to examine the suitability of a membrane 24-27 for a proposed gas separation problem since with rather simple potential models we have be able to capture all the qualitative details of these separations. This can significantly reduce the number of experimental studies necessary in designing or discovering a membrane for a new separation process. Results also show surface diffusion generally dominates the permeation at low temperature. For mixture components with similar size and adsorption characteristics, such as O 2 / N 2 , small-pore size zeolites are not appropriate. This is because molecules cannot easily pass each other in the narrow pore system. In mixtures with components with different adsorption and diffusion behavior such as CO2/N2, separation is mainly governed by adsorption. The lower the adsorption of molecule, the lower the permeation rate, even if the diffusion rate is higher. External elec- tric fields do not appear to improve the permeance of gases through the membrane or increase the separation factors of the systems studied here.

Results also show surface diffusion generally dominates the permeation at low temperature. For mixture components with similar size and adsorption characteristics, such as O 2 / N 2 , small-pore size zeolites are not appropriate. This is because molecules cannot easily pass each other in the narrow pore system. In mixtures with components with different adsorption and diffusion behavior such as CO2/N2, separation is mainly governed by adsorption. The lower the adsorption of molecule, the lower the permeation rate, even if the diffusion rate is higher. External elec- tric fields do not appear to improve the permeance of gases through the membrane or increase the separation factors of the systems studied here.
